dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 for a Transfer/Interfacility/Palliative Care call at the Richmond clinic.
Hi, I'm Taylor, an EMT with emergency services. You're in the clinic exam room-what brought you to the center today?
This is a Transfer/Interfacility/Palliative Care call-we'll ensure you're stable during transport. Any notable pain or concerns?
I've been short of breath, especially when getting up or walking a few steps. Also, I've had muscle and joint pain.
Thank you for that. Was this shortness of breath first noticed today, or earlier?
We note you're on oxygen at 4 LPM-has this helped with breathing?
You also mentioned muscle and joint pain. Have you noticed chest pain along with the SOB?
No chest pain. Just SOB when I move around. No discomfort in the chest.
Let's assess your responsiveness. Can you squeeze both of my fingers tightly?
(Squeezes fingers) That's about the best I can do.
That was firm-good. I want to check your alertness: tell me your name, where we are, the date, and why you're here.
I'm Alice Johnson. I'm at a clinic in Richmond. Today is November 6th. I was here for muscle and joint pain and became short of breath.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15-fully alert and oriented.
You are A&Ox4-alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
At the clinic, your O2 saturation was in the 80s-it improved with 4 LPM oxygen. Does this improve your symptoms while seated?
Yes-when I'm seated and on oxygen, I breathe more easily. But once I stand, it gets worse.
You also have a history of sarcoidosis, CHF, and possibly pulmonary hypertension. That noted, do you usually get crackles in the base of your lungs?
At 11:16, your vitals were 157/108, 122 bpm, 21 breaths per minute-your blood pressure and heart rate are elevated.
These vitals were taken when you were already awake and in normal condition (NAD) with no signs of immediate distress or unstable state.
SpO₂ was 96% on 4 LPM via nasal cannula-good improvement from baseline.
Blood pressure is 157 over 108-elevated, but not unexpected.
Pulse is 122-very fast. Your heart is working hard.
Your history includes sarcoidosis, CHF, and COPD. Recently, are you noticing more shortness of breath or leg swelling?
You're on Diltiazem, DuoNeb, Lasix, Potassium, Warfarin, and Vitamin B12. Are you taking them as prescribed?
Yes, with help from my wife, I don't miss a dose.
At the clinic, your blood glucose was 119-normal.
No significant findings in skin such as bruises, rashes, or scars noted. Any new skin changes with pain reported?
No-my skin looks normal. I don't see anything unusual.
No swelling or lumps near the neck observed. I'll continue the physical exam.
Updated vitals: 160/103 mmHg, 122 bpm, 27 breaths per minute-your respiration rate has increased.
Our ECG shows supraventricular tachycardia at 122 bpm with ST elevation in inferior leads-no ST elevations in posterior leads.
This EKG finding raises concern for possible STEMI with posterior extension based on changes in V1-V3.
Per normal treatment protocol, I've placed an 18-gauge IV in the left arm for a saline lock-done successfully.
Your initial respiratory rate was 21, but now it's 27-elevated and potentially significant beyond typical COPD concerns.
A 4-lead ECG struggled to obtain a signal. Let me try the 12-lead again with the posterior leads-final attempt.
I randomized a second glucose test, but no value was recorded-likely equipment interference.
324 mg of aspirin has been given by mouth, per standing orders. Assistance with water is available to help you swallow it.
Let me repeat the 12-lead ECG during transport to confirm findings. Please stay still if you can.
Repeat EKG shows STE in the inferior leads with sinus tachycardia and right axis deviation-supporting the STEMI-ACS/AMI field impression.
IV site is secure, no swelling or abnormalities in the extremities.
We will assist you using a sheet lift with a five-person team to help you onto the stretcher.
We are proceeding to VCU Health Systems for ED admission with recognition of STEMI-ACS/AMI conditions.
All assessments and treatments completed. Ready for safe transport per protocol 2-2.
Rechecking vitals: 160/103 mmHg, 122 bpm, 27 breaths per minute-stable with slight increases.
SpO₂ remains at 95% on oxygen-no significant change.
Since you rated earlier on a scale of 0-10 as a 5, does your discomfort feel the same now?
It's still about a 5-no sharp or chest pain. It's just uncomfortable.
Again, we note sarcoidosis, CHF, COPD, and possibly pulmonary hypertension. Any worsening symptoms in the last 24 hours?
Are you current on all prescriptions-Diltiazem, Lasix, Duoneb, and so on?
Yes. My wife assists with these medications, so I take them daily as prescribed.
Movement is secure. We will watch your breathing and report to the cardiac team.
We are heading to VCU for cardiology evaluation related to the STEMI finding.
All steps complete. Transporting now, per protocol 2-2.
